sections.title: API Design Foundations: REST, GraphQL, gRPC & When to Use Each
sections.description:
FOUNDATIONS
ParadigmStrengthsWeaknessesBest For
RESTCacheable, stateless, uniform interfaceOver-fetching, chatty, no strong typingCRUD-heavy, web-facing APIs
GraphQLDeclarative fetching, single endpoint, strong typingComplex caching, N+1 risk, query costDynamic UIs, aggregating sources
gRPCBinary perf, streaming, strong contractsBrowser unfriendly, tooling maturityMicroservice <-> microservice
A2A / Agent-to-AgentStandardized delegation, async coordination, discoveryLatency overhead, OAuth2/identity token complexityMulti-agent workflows, inter-org systems
WebTransportLow latency (QUIC), multiplexed streams, unreliable modesDraft spec, limited proxy/firewall supportReal-time streaming, edge-to-server bidirection
Staff+ Interview Tip: Never present one protocol as universally superior. Frame your answer around trade-offs: team expertise, ecosystem constraints, traffic patterns, and operational maturity.
Hybrid Patterns: Many orgs expose REST externally and gRPC internally. GraphQL can sit as a gateway layer over both. Discuss strangler fig migrations when shifting between protocols.
A2A Protocol Mechanics: A2A (Agent-to-Agent) and MCP (Model Context Protocol) utilize JSON-RPC 2.0 or Server-Sent Events (SSE) to facilitate discovery, dynamic capabilities listing, and schema-governed tool calling, decoupling the agent layer from internal backend implementations.

sections.title: API-First Design: Contracts, Specs & Code Generation
sections.description:
API-FIRST

API-first design means the contract is the source of truth — code is generated from the spec, not the other way around. This approach enables parallel development, automated testing, and self-documenting APIs that stay in sync.

Spec AuthoringCode GenerationContract TestingMock Servers
LayerToolingPurpose
OpenAPI 3.1, AsyncAPI, GraphQL SDL, ProtobufDefine endpoints, schemas, errors, auth flows before implementation
OpenAPI Generator, protoc, graphql-codegenGenerate server stubs, client SDKs, typed resolvers, mocks
Pact, Dredd, Optic, SpectralEnforce spec compliance, detect breaking changes in CI
Prism, Mockoon, WireMockUnblock frontend & integration teams before backend is ready
Staff+ Interview Tip: Emphasize that contracts are a governance boundary. A spec review board (with breaking-change checklists) is a common pattern at large orgs. Discuss how you'd enforce contracts across dozens of teams.
Schema Evolution: Protobuf uses field numbers (never reuse/rename). OpenAPI 3.1 uses JSON Schema 2020-12 for composable schemas. GraphQL uses deprecation directives and schema registry for gradual migration.
Generation Pipelines: At Staff+ level, design a CI pipeline that: lint → breaking-change detection → mock generation → contract tests → publish to registry. This is the intersection of API design and platform engineering.

sections.title: Versioning Strategies: Breaking vs Non-Breaking Changes
sections.description:
VERSIONING
URI PathHeaderQuery ParamContent Negotiation
StrategyMechanismProsCons
/v1/customersExplicit, cache-friendlyCode duplication, slower evolution
Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=2Clean URLs, no duplicationHidden from casual inspection
?version=2Easy to testPollutes URLs, caching issues
Accept header with media typeRESTful, leverages HTTPComplex client logic
Non-Breaking Changes: Adding optional fields, adding enum values (if clients ignore unknowns), expanding response payloads, relaxing constraints (e.g., making a required field optional). Always use tolerant reader / Postel's Law on the server side.
Breaking Changes: Removing/renaming fields, changing field types, making optional → required, changing enum values, removing endpoints, changing auth requirements. Detect these with Optic or Spectral in CI.
Staff+ Decision: Prefer compatibility-first versioning over aggressive version bumps. Each version is a tax — you must maintain, test, document, and support it. At big orgs, version lifetimes are measured in years. Design a deprecation policy (e.g., N-2 support, sunset headers, migration guides).

sections.title: Pagination, Filtering & Sorting at Scale
sections.description:
PAGINATION
Cursor-basedOffset-basedKeyset / SeekPage tokens
PatternHow It WorksWhen to Use
opaque cursor + limit (cursor=abc123)Real-time data, infinite scroll, high consistency
offset + limit (?offset=20&limit=10)Simple, stable datasets, random access
WHERE id > ? ORDER BY id LIMIT ?High throughput, no offset overhead
Encoded cursor in opaque tokenDistributed backends, hides implementation

At Staff+ level, discuss how pagination interacts with consistency. Cursor-based pagination avoids offset drift (items inserted/deleted between pages), but requires a stable sort order. Keyset pagination is the most performant for large datasets but breaks with non-unique sort columns.

Filtering: Expose filters as query parameters with a consistent syntax: ?status=active&created_at.gt=2024-01-01. For complex filtering, consider a $filter DSL (OData, Searchkit) but beware of SQL injection at the API layer.
Sorting: Multi-field sort: ?sort=-created_at,name (minus for descending). Limit sort fields to indexed columns to prevent full table scans. GraphQL provides orderBy arguments; gRPC uses a sort field enum in the request message.
Edge Cases: Paginating across sharded databases requires scatter-gather or global index. Real-time feeds (activity logs, notifications) demand cursor-based. Ask your interviewer: "What are the consistency requirements for this list?" before proposing a strategy.

sections.title: Rate Limiting, Throttling & Quota Management
sections.description:
RATE LIMITING
Token BucketLeaky BucketSliding WindowFixed WindowConcurrency
AlgorithmBehaviorUse Case
Tokens refill at fixed rate, burst capacityGeneral purpose, bursty traffic
Requests processed at fixed rate, queue overflowSmoothing traffic spikes
Count requests in rolling time windowPrecise, distributed-friendly
Reset counter at interval boundariesSimple but thundering herd at reset
Max in-flight requests at onceProtecting backend resources
Headers: Return X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset. Use Retry-After on 429 responses. Consistency in rate-limit headers is a hallmark of production-grade APIs.
Quota Management: Distinguish between rate limits (requests/second) and quotas (requests/day/month). Quotas are enforced asynchronously — track usage in a counter DB (Redis, Cassandra) and alert before hard cutoff.
Distributed Rate Limiting: Use Redis Cluster + Lua scripting for atomic sliding windows. For global limits, a consensus-based approach (CRDT counters) avoids a single point of failure. Discuss eventual consistency trade-offs for quota enforcement.

sections.title: Authentication & Authorization Patterns (OAuth2, JWT, API Keys)
sections.description:
AUTH
API Keys (Bearer token)JWT (self-contained)OAuth2 + opaque tokensSession cookiemTLS
MechanismStateless?Best For
Yes (with validation)Server-to-server, internal tools
YesDistributed systems, no session store
No (token introspection)Third-party delegation, user context
NoBrowser-based apps, legacy systems
N/AZero-trust, service mesh

At Staff+ level, design for the token lifecycle: issuance, refresh, revocation, and rotation. JWT has convenience but introduces key rotation, clock skew, and payload size concerns. OAuth2 with opaque tokens shifts introspection cost to the auth server but enables instant revocation.

OAuth2 Flows: Authorization Code + PKCE for SPAs, Client Credentials for machine-to-machine, Device Authorization for CLI/headless. Discuss token exchange patterns (RFC 8693) for multi-service delegation.
Authorization: RBAC (role-based) is coarse; ABAC (attribute-based) via OPA/rego or Casbin supports fine-grained policies. At scale, use a PDP (Policy Decision Point) separated from the PEP (Policy Enforcement Point) in the API gateway.
Staff+ Interview Tip: Draw a sequence diagram showing: client → gateway (token validation) → PDP (policy check) → upstream service (scoped context). Discuss how you'd handle token revocation with a distributed bloom filter or a bounded revocation list.

sections.title: Error Handling & Idempotency
sections.description:
ERRORS
400 Bad Request401 / 403404 Not Found409 Conflict429 Too Many Requests500 Internal Server Error502 / 503 / 504
Status CodeSemanticsRetryable?
Client error (validation, bad body)No — fix request first
Unauthenticated / UnauthorizedNo (refresh token if 401)
Resource doesn't existNo
Version mismatch, duplicateMaybe — depends on cause
Rate limit exceededYes — with Retry-After
Server-side faultYes — with exponential backoff
Gateway / unavailable / timeoutYes — with backoff & jitter
Standard Error Body: {"error": {"code": "VALIDATION_ERROR", "message": "...", "details": [...], "trace_id": "..."}}. Include a machine-readable code, human-readable message, and a trace_id for debugging.
Idempotency Key: Use Idempotency-Key header for POST/PATCH. The server deduplicates within a TTL window (e.g., 24h). Store the key → response mapping in a durable DB. Return the original response on replay — critical for payment APIs.
Staff+ Design: Error codes should be hierarchical: PAYMENT.DECLINED.INSUFFICIENT_FUNDS. Publish a machine-readable error catalog. For idempotency, consider optimistic concurrency via If-Match / ETag alongside idempotency keys.

sections.title: API Security: Input Validation, Rate Limits, CORS & Beyond
sections.description:
SECURITY
TransportInputAuthOutputCORS
LayerControlImplementation
TLS 1.3, HSTS, Certificate pinningEnforce at load balancer / gateway
Schema validation, allow-listing, length limitsValidate JSON against OpenAPI spec (e.g., express-validator, jsonschema)
Rate-limit login endpoints, account lockoutPer-IP + per-account rate limiting
Response filtering, no stack tracesStrip internal fields, normalize error payloads
Allow-list origins, not wildcardValidate Origin header, preflight caching
Input Validation Depth: Validate at the API gateway (syntactic) AND the service (semantic). Guard against mass assignment (use DTOs, never bind directly to entities). Limit request body size (e.g., 10 MB) and field depth (e.g., max 6 levels of nesting).
OWASP Top 10 for APIs: Broken object-level authorization (BOLA/IDOR), excessive data exposure, mass assignment, injection, security misconfiguration. API-specific tooling: 42Crunch, Salt Security, APISec.
Staff+ Strategy: Design a defense-in-depth posture: WAF → gateway auth → rate limiting → schema validation → service authorization → output sanitization. Every layer fails closed. Implement a security chaos engineering practice (fuzz endpoints, inject faults).

sections.title: Performance: Caching, Compression, Connection Pooling
sections.description:
PERFORMANCE
HTTP CachingCDN CachingApplication CacheCompressionConnection PoolingBatching
TechniqueMechanismImpact
Cache-Control, ETag, If-None-MatchReduces origin load, improves latency
Edge caching for GET responsesGlobal latency reduction, shield origin
Redis / Memcached for computed dataSub-millisecond reads for hot data
gzip / brotli (accept-encoding)60-80% bandwidth reduction
HTTP keep-alive, DB connection poolReduces handshake overhead, TCP port reuse
GraphQL batching, gRPC streamingFewer round-trips, amortizes overhead
Cache Invalidation: The hardest problem in computer science. Use TTL-based (time-based) + event-driven (cache-aside with write-through). For distributed caches, consider write-behind with a queue for high throughput.
GraphQL Caching: POST requests are not cacheable by default. Use Automatic Persisted Queries (APQ) to turn queries into GET requests. Consider a CDN cache for persisted query IDs. Use a DataLoader to batch and cache at the resolver level.
Staff+ Design: Measure first — profile P50/P95/P99 latencies. Use latency budgets per API call. At scale, implement hedged requests (race two backends) or request coalescing (deduplicate concurrent identical requests at the proxy layer).

sections.title: API Observability: Metrics, Tracing, Logging
sections.description:
OBSERVABILITY
Metrics (RED)Distributed TracingStructured LoggingProfilingAlerting
PillarWhat It Tells YouTooling
Rate, Errors, DurationPrometheus + Grafana dashboards
Request flow across services, bottlenecksOpenTelemetry + Jaeger / Tempo
Debugging, audit trail, pattern analysisJSON logs, ELK / Loki + Grafana
CPU, memory, I/O hot spotspy-spy, pprof, async-profiler
Anomaly detection, SLO burn rateAlertmanager, PagerDuty, OpsGenie
Correlation IDs: Every request gets a X-Request-ID (client-generated) or X-Trace-ID (gateway-generated). Propagate via headers (W3C Trace-Context format). This connects logs, traces, and metrics for a single request lifecycle.
SLO / SLI / SLA: Define Service Level Indicators (e.g., latency P99 < 500ms), Objectives (99.9% of requests), and Agreements (contractual). Use burn-rate alerting to detect SLO violations early.
Staff+ Interview Tip: Draw the three pillars and show how they connect: a Prometheus metric alert → links to a Grafana dashboard → drill into a trace in Jaeger → view structured logs for that trace_id. This closed-loop observability is expected at Staff+ level.

sections.title: GraphQL Deep Dive: Schema Design, N+1, DataLoader Patterns
sections.description:
GRAPHQL
Schema-firstDataLoaderCursor ConnectionsDataloader per-requestQuery Cost Analysis
ConceptPatternWhy It Matters
SDL defines types, queries, mutations firstContract-driven, self-documenting, tooling (GraphiQL, introspection)
Batch + cache function per entity typeEliminates N+1 — coalesces DB/DATA requests per tick
Relay spec: edges, node, pageInfoStandardized pagination, stable cursors, totalCount?
New DataLoader per HTTP requestIsolates cache to request lifecycle, prevents stale data
Complexity limit, depth limit, rate limitingPrevents abusive queries that overload the backend
N+1 Problem: Resolver fetches a list, then each item triggers a separate fetch. DataLoader coalesces these into a single batch. At Staff+ level, discuss lookahead patterns — use info to precompute join paths and avoid lazy loading entirely.
Schema Design Heuristics: Prefer connections for lists (Relay spec), unions for polymorphic results, interfaces for shared fields. Mutations return a MutationResponse union with success and errors for client-side handling.
Federation: Apollo Federation v2 / GraphQL Mesh for composing schemas across services. Discuss entity resolution (@key), computed fields (@requires), and cross-service auth context propagation. This is a GraphQL microservice architecture.

sections.title: gRPC & Protobuf: Streaming, Bidirectional, Interceptors
sections.description:
gRPC
UnaryServer StreamingClient StreamingBidirectional
RPC TypeDirectionUse Case
Client → Server → ClientStandard request-response
Client sends one, server streams backEvent feeds, log tailing, progress updates
Client streams, server responds onceFile upload, telemetry batches
Both sides stream independentlyReal-time chat, collaborative editing, live dashboards
Protobuf Best Practices: Never reuse field numbers. Reserve deleted fields with reserved. Use google.protobuf.Timestamp (not string). Enums should have a UNSPECIFIED = 0 sentinel. Use wrappers.proto for nullable primitives (though optional is now proto3 native).
Interceptors: gRPC middleware — auth (JWT extraction), logging (request/response payloads), rate limiting, circuit breaking, tracing. Implement as unary interceptors (simple) and stream interceptors (stream wrapping). At scale, interceptors replace API gateway functions for service-to-service calls.
gRPC Gateway: Expose gRPC services as REST/JSON via grpc-gateway or envoy. Annotations in protos generate REST endpoints. This is the single source of truth approach — write once (proto) and publish multiple protocol faces.

sections.title: Event-Driven APIs: Webhooks, SSE, Event Sourcing
sections.description:
EVENT-DRIVEN
WebhooksServer-Sent Events (SSE)Event SourcingWebSocket
PatternDirectionDeliveryUse Case
Server → Client (push)HTTP POST to registered URL, retry with backoffAsync notifications: payment success, CI complete, deploy status
Server → Client (stream)Single long-lived HTTP connection, auto-reconnectLive feeds: notifications, stock ticker, log tailing
Append-only event logEvents are the source of truth; current state is derivedAudit trails, temporal queries, undo/replay
Bidirectional full-duplexPersistent TCP connection after HTTP upgradeReal-time collaboration, gaming, live dashboards
Webhook Reliability: Implement at-least-once delivery with idempotency keys (X-Idempotency-Key). Exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s... max 1h). Dead-letter queue after N retries. Webhook signatures (HMAC-SHA256) for authenticity.
Event Sourcing + CQRS: Commands → Event Store (append-only, e.g., EventStoreDB, Kafka) → Projections (materialized views). APIs read from projections. At Staff+ level, discuss event versioning (schema registry for events), tolerance for replay, and eventual consistency semantics.
Staff+ Trade-off Discussion: Webhooks vs polling — webhooks push (real-time, less load) but add delivery complexity; polling pull (simpler server, client-driven). SSE is simpler than WebSockets for one-way server-to-client. Choose based on client complexity tolerance and volume.

sections.title: Internal vs External APIs: Design for Different Consumers
sections.description:
INTERNAL v EXTERNAL
VersioningAuthContractSLADeprecationDocumentation
ConcernInternal APIExternal (Public) API
Semver with rapid iterationCalendar versioning, extended support windows
mTLS, service tokens, VPCOAuth2, API keys, rate-limited per customer
Protobuf, Thrift, internal OpenAPIStable OpenAPI, SDK generation, changelogs
Best-effort or internal SLOsContractual SLAs with penalties
1 quarter notice12-18 months notice, sunset headers, migration guides
Proto docs, in-house wikiPublic developer portal, tutorials, Postman collections
BFF Pattern (Backend for Frontend): A dedicated API layer per client type (web BFF, mobile BFF, IoT BFF). Each BFF owns the contract for its consumer. This prevents shared APIs from being contaminated by client-specific concerns (e.g., mobile needing sparse fields, web needing rich fragments).
API Products: Treat external APIs as products. Assign a product manager, publish a roadmap, maintain a changelog, and provide support channels. Implement a developer portal (SwaggerHub, Redocly, Backstage) for onboarding.
Staff+ Governance: Design an API review board process: spec review → breaking change assessment → security audit → developer experience review → publish. Automate compliance checks in CI. Balance speed (internal) vs stability (external) with a tiered API maturity model.

sections.title: API Gateway & Service Mesh Patterns
sections.description:
GATEWAY
API Gateway (sidecar)Service Mesh (sidecar)Ingress GatewayBFF Gateway
PatternResponsibilitiesExamples
Auth, rate limit, routing, transformation, cachingKong, Tyk, Apigee, AWS API Gateway, Zuul
Service-to-service: mTLS, retry, circuit break, tracingIstio, Linkerd, Consul Connect, Envoy
External entry point into meshIstio Ingress, Contour, Traefik
Per-client aggregation and transformationGraphQL gateway, custom BFF layer
Gateway Responsibilities: Authentication (OAuth2 / JWT validation), rate limiting, request/response transformation, routing to upstream services, caching (GET responses), CORS enforcement, request logging, and circuit breaking. A gateway offloads cross-cutting concerns from services.
Service Mesh Value: Provides mTLS (zero-trust encryption), traffic splitting (canary, blue-green), circuit breaking (envoy outlier detection), distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry), and telemetry (TCP-level metrics). Sidecar proxies manage this transparently.
Staff+ Architecture Decision: Gateway vs Mesh — gateways handle north-south traffic (external → internal), meshes handle east-west (service → service). You need both at scale. Discuss gateway chaining (ingress → BFF → mesh → service) and latency overhead of proxy hops.

sections.title: API Design Interview Rubric & Sample Questions
sections.description:
INTERVIEW RUBRIC
Requirements GatheringTrade-off AnalysisSchema DesignProtocol ChoiceSecurity & AuthScalabilityError HandlingCommunication
DimensionBar (Staff)Strong (Senior Staff)
Asks clarifying questions, defines scopeChallenges ambiguous requirements, identifies implicit constraints, prioritizes by impact
Compares 2-3 optionsSystematic trade-off matrix, cites real-world examples from well-known APIs
Logical resource model, standard namingEvolvable schema, versioning strategy, backward compatibility baked in
Justifies REST / GraphQL / gRPC choiceHybrid protocol strategy, migration path, consumer-specific interfaces
Covers authN/Z basicsDefense-in-depth, token lifecycle, OWASP API-specific threats
Mentions caching, pagination, rate limitsQuantified load estimates, bottleneck analysis, horizontal scaling strategy
Standard error format, status codesIdempotency, retry strategy, error catalog, client guidance
Clear explanations, whiteboard sketchesStructured thinking, adapts to interviewer signals, asks for feedback
Sample Question 1: "Design a REST API for a collaborative document editing service (like Google Docs)." Focus on resource model (docs, revisions, comments, presence), real-time sync (WebSocket + operational transforms), permissions, and conflict resolution.
Sample Question 2: "Design a payment processing API." Cover idempotency keys, webhooks (async confirmation), error handling (insufficient funds, declined), idempotency replay protection, and PCI compliance scope.
Sample Question 3: "Design a rate-limiting service API." Discuss configurable rate limit policies (per-consumer, per-endpoint), token bucket vs sliding window, multi-region replication, and coordination between gateway and services.
Sample Question 4: "Design a notification API." Cover push (FCM/APNS), pull (polling/SSE), webhooks, template management, delivery guarantees (at-least-once), unsubscribes, and delivery status tracking.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
REFERENCES
Books: API Design Patterns (JJ Geewax), The Design of Web APIs (Arnaud Lauret), RESTful Web APIs (Richardson & Amundsen), Building Microservices (Sam Newman).
Specifications: OpenAPI 3.1 (https://spec.openapis.org/oas/v3.1.0), AsyncAPI 2.x (https://www.asyncapi.com), GraphQL June 2018 Spec (https://spec.graphql.org), Protocol Buffers v3 (https://protobuf.dev).
Standards & RFCs: HTTP Semantics (RFC 9110), HTTP Caching (RFC 9111), OAuth 2.0 (RFC 6749), JWT (RFC 7519), Idempotency (RFC 7231 §4.2.2), Problem Details (RFC 9457).
Tools: OpenAPI Generator, Stoplight Spectral, Optic, Prism, GraphiQL, Apollo Studio, grpcurl, Envoy, Istio, Kong, Tyk, OpenTelemetry, Prometheus, Grafana, Jaeger.
